mapQuestions.question: Which continent is Serbia on?
mapQuestions.answer: Europe, in the centre of the Balkan Peninsula. Serbia is landlocked, surrounded by Hungary, Romania, Bulgaria, North Macedonia, Kosovo, Montenegro, Bosnia and Herzegovina, and Croatia 🌍

mapQuestions.question: What is the capital of Serbia?
mapQuestions.answer: Belgrade (Beograd in Serbian, meaning "white city"). About 1.4 million people, sitting where the Sava river flows into the giant Danube. A historic crossing point of Europe.

mapQuestions.question: What language does Serbia speak?
mapQuestions.answer: Serbian, which is essentially the same language as Bosnian and Croatian. Serbia uses both Cyrillic (official) and Latin scripts, often on the same street signs.

mapQuestions.question: How important is the Danube to Serbia?
mapQuestions.answer: The Danube River cuts right through the middle of Serbia for 588 km. Belgrade sits where the Sava meets the Danube. The river has carried trade, armies and ideas across central Europe for over 3,000 years.

mapQuestions.question: What is the most famous Serbian inventor?
mapQuestions.answer: Nikola Tesla, born in 1856 in what is now Croatia to ethnic Serbian parents. He invented the AC electrical system that powers most of the world today. Belgrade Airport is named after him and his face is on the 100-dinar banknote.

foods.name: Ćevapi
foods.description: Small grilled minced-meat fingers served with somun flatbread, raw chopped onion and kajmak. Shared across the Balkans, but Serbs will tell you Leskovac in southern Serbia makes the best.

foods.name: Pljeskavica
foods.description: A flat Balkan burger patty of mixed minced meat, grilled over coals, stuffed into flatbread with cabbage, onion, ajvar and kajmak. Pljeskavica from Leskovac is legendary.

foods.name: Burek
foods.description: Flaky filo-pastry pie filled with cheese, meat, or spinach, sold by the slice at every bakery from early morning. Serbs eat it with a glass of cold yogurt.

foods.name: Gibanica
foods.description: A layered baked filo pie with feta-style cheese and eggs between every paper-thin layer. The Serbian breakfast pie.

foods.name: Ajvar
foods.description: A bright-red relish made from roasted red peppers and aubergine, slow-cooked into a sweet-smoky spread. Eaten with bread, cheese, grilled meat, basically everything. Made by families in autumn.

foods.name: Vanilice
foods.description: Small sandwich biscuits filled with jam and dusted with icing sugar. The classic Serbian celebration cookie, made for Christmas, Slava family-saint days, and weddings.

scavengerItems.label: Belgrade's Kalemegdan fortress, where the Sava meets the Danube
A gold-domed Serbian Orthodox church (St Sava temple in Belgrade is one of the biggest Orthodox churches in the world)
The Danube and Sava rivers meeting in front of the fortress
A Belgrade tram (and almost no metro, the city is on the Danube floodplain)
The Petrovaradin fortress in Novi Sad (with EXIT music festival each July)
A brass band playing at a square or kafana (Serbian-style restaurant)
Plums hanging on a tree (Serbia is one of the world's biggest plum producers)
A small medieval Orthodox monastery painted in colourful frescoes (Studenica, Sopoćani, Žiča)
A small Turkish-style coffee in a copper pot, just like Bosnian (called "domaća kafa" or "Serbian coffee" here)
Cyclists on the Belgrade Danube quay path
Brown bear tracks in Tara National Park (Serbia has a small but real bear population)
A wedding band playing fast Balkan-trumpet music in a village square

geoMatches.description: The capital. Sits high on a bluff where the Sava meets the Danube. The Kalemegdan fortress, busy old town, riverboat bars ("splavovi"), and one of Europe's most energetic nightlife scenes.
Serbia's second city, north of Belgrade. Home to the Petrovaradin Fortress (where the EXIT music festival happens each July), a relaxed Habsburg-influenced old town and the Danube cycle path.
A 12th-century Serbian Orthodox monastery in central Serbia. White marble walls, vivid medieval frescoes, and UNESCO World Heritage. Still a working monastery with monks living on site.
A national park in western Serbia along a deep canyon of the Drina river. Spruce forests, hiking trails, and the famous "lonely house on a rock" on a tiny river island.
A surprising Art-Nouveau city on the Hungarian border. Pastel-coloured turn-of-the-century buildings, including a stunning synagogue.
Serbia's third city, in the south. Birthplace of the Roman emperor Constantine the Great (the first Christian emperor) in 272 AD. Roman ruins and a strong food culture.

senses.smell: Smoke from a ćevapi grill, sweet ripe plums in autumn, fresh-baked burek from a Belgrade bakery at 6am, woodsmoke from village houses, the deep green smell of the Tara forests…
senses.hear: "Zdravo!" "Hvala!", brass-band wedding music from a courtyard, the Sava-Danube meeting under fortress walls, Cyrillic and Latin script signs everywhere, the Belgrade tram rattling past Kalemegdan…
senses.taste: Smoky grilled ćevapi with chopped onion, sweet-smoky ajvar pepper relish, soft-cheesy gibanica pie, dense bitter Serbian coffee, sweet vanilice cookies dusted with icing sugar…
senses.feel: Cobblestones in Belgrade's Skadarlija bohemian street, the cool spray of the Tara river, the smooth marble walls of Studenica monastery, the buzz of Belgrade nightlife, the deep stone of a Roman ruin in Niš…
senses.see: What is the most incredible thing right in front of you right now…

stories.question: Why is Belgrade called "the white city"?
stories.body: "Beograd" literally means "white city" in Serbian. The name probably came from the limestone-and-chalk cliffs on which the medieval fortress of Kalemegdan stands, gleaming white above the Sava and Danube rivers below. Belgrade has been built, destroyed, and rebuilt around 40 times throughout its history, fought over by Romans, Byzantines, Ottomans, Habsburgs, Serbians, and many more. The current city is mostly post-WWII, with patches of older architecture surviving in pockets. The combination of long history, riverside setting, and reputation as Europe's most energetic night-time scene gives Belgrade a unique character: ancient and modern in the same breath.

stories.question: What are Serbian Orthodox monasteries?
stories.body: Tucked away in remote Serbian valleys are dozens of medieval Orthodox monasteries: Studenica, Sopoćani, Žiča, Gračanica, Mileševa, Manasija. Most date from the 1100s to 1300s, when Serbia was a powerful medieval kingdom. Inside, the walls are covered in vivid Byzantine-style frescoes (the "Studenica madonna", the famous "White Angel" at Mileševa) that have survived 800 years almost intact. Monks still live in most of these monasteries today, keeping the same routines (prayer, gardening, beekeeping, candle-making) that have been kept for centuries. UNESCO has listed several of them as World Heritage sites. Visiting feels like stepping into a quiet, ancient Serbia that runs alongside the modern country.

stories.question: Who was Nikola Tesla?
stories.body: Nikola Tesla was born in 1856 in a village called Smiljan, in what was then the Austrian Empire (and is now Croatia), to ethnic Serbian parents. He moved to America in 1884 and became one of the most important inventors in modern history. He invented alternating current (AC) electricity, which is what powers your house right now. He invented the AC induction motor, wireless transmission of electricity (the basis of modern radio), and dozens of devices that shaped the 20th century. His rival was Thomas Edison, who pushed direct current. Tesla's AC system eventually won, and it changed the world. Tesla is celebrated as a Serbian national hero. Belgrade's airport is named after him, his ashes are kept in a museum in central Belgrade, and his face is on the 100-dinar banknote.
stories.thinkingQuestion: One inventor's ideas now power almost every house on Earth. Whose work do you think shapes our daily lives without us noticing?

stories.question: What does Serbia remember from the 1990s?
stories.body: When Yugoslavia broke apart in the early 1990s, Serbia was at the centre of a difficult and painful decade. Wars in Croatia, Bosnia and later Kosovo involved many countries and many sides, and a huge amount of suffering on all of them. In 1999, NATO bombed Belgrade and other Serbian cities for 78 days during the Kosovo war. Marks of that period are still visible on some buildings in central Belgrade. Modern Serbia is at peace, has rebuilt enormously, and Belgrade is now one of Europe's most energetic young capitals. Serbs are warm, generous hosts, and welcome visitors openly. The country's 1990s history is complicated and still painful for many people who lived through it, and visiting respectfully means being willing to listen to a range of views from people who experienced it first-hand.

stories.question: Who invented the trumpet style of "Balkan brass"?
stories.body: Drive through southern Serbia in the summer and you might hear it before you see it: fast, joyful, slightly chaotic trumpet music played at top volume. This is "Balkan brass", which grew out of Serbian Romani (Roma) brass bands in the 1800s and was made world-famous by composers and bandleaders like Goran Bregović and Boban Marković. Every August the small town of Guča hosts the world's biggest brass-band festival ("Sabor"), drawing hundreds of bands and tens of thousands of fans. Films like Emir Kusturica's "Underground" and "Black Cat, White Cat" gave Balkan brass a global audience. It is impossible to listen to without smiling.

convoQuestions.question: Best food in Serbia so far?
Belgrade, Novi Sad, monastery, mountain, which has felt the most "Serbia"?
How does Belgrade feel different from other European capitals?
Did you spot Cyrillic AND Latin script on the same sign?
Serbia is still rebuilding from a hard 1990s. How does meeting people who lived through that change how you feel about a place?

wordSearch.hint: Serbian foods, places and things to spot.

animals.name: Wild boar
animals.description: Common across Serbian forests, especially in Fruška Gora and around the Tara canyon. Sometimes wander into village outskirts in winter.

animals.name: Red deer
animals.description: In the Tara, Kopaonik and Đerdap national parks. The autumn rutting roar is unforgettable.

animals.name: Brown bear
animals.description: Serbia's small bear population lives mostly in the Tara National Park along the Drina canyon.

animals.name: Griffon vulture
animals.description: A huge vulture with a 2.8 m wingspan. Nesting colonies on the cliffs of Uvac canyon in south-western Serbia.

animals.name: Grey wolf
animals.description: Returning to Serbian mountains, especially the Carpathians along the Romanian border.

animals.name: European beaver
animals.description: Re-introduced to the Sava and other Serbian rivers after going extinct locally. Beavers are now thriving.

animals.name: Eurasian lynx
animals.description: A very small lynx population in the south-eastern mountains. Almost impossible to see.
